chunk_summary: Caregiver misconceptions about texture progression in complementary feeding (6–24 months) can delay dietary adequacy. Addressing myths about digestion, choking, and food choices promotes safe texture progression, nutrient-dense meals, and proper feeding practices.
learning_objectives: Understand common caregiver misconceptions that delay texture progression in complementary feeding.
Learn strategies to address myths about choking, digestion, and food choices in infants and toddlers.
Promote safe texture progression and nutrient-dense dietary practices by 9–10 months of age.
key_facts: Thick, spoonable foods are recommended starting at 6 months to fill the energy gap without displacing milk feeds.
Lumpy textures should be introduced by 9–10 months to prevent feeding delays; gagging is common and protective, while choking is rare with proper supervision.
Ultra-processed snacks like biscuits displace nutrient-dense foods and may pose choking hazards; replace with snacks like fruit, curd, egg, or dal-based options.
Juice adds free sugars, reduces appetite for meals, and increases caries risk; water and milk are the recommended beverages for children under two years.
Scheduled meals, a safe texture ladder, and daily iron-rich foods are key to achieving dietary adequacy in complementary feeding.
reference_quote: At 6 months, solids do not mean liquids. Thick, spoonable foods are recommended and help fill the energy gap without displacing milk feeds.
document_key_topics: Texture progression and developmental feeding windows
Nutrient-dense food choices for infants and toddlers
